entities.name: HP Action for Repairs and Services
entities.description: Tenants may start a lawsuit against their landlords based on the landlord's failure to make repairs or provide essential services.

entities.name: Rent Reduction for Decreased Services
entities.description: Tenants in rent-stabilized or rent-controlled apartments can file for a rent reduction based on decreased services.

entities.name: Retaliatory Eviction Defense
entities.description: If a landlord refuses to renew a lease or gives a significant rent increase within a year of a repair request, it could be considered retaliation.

entities.name: Breach of Warranty of Habitability
entities.description: Tenants have a right to withhold rent and may be entitled to a rent abatement if they can document not getting repairs or essential services.

entities.name: Written Letter to Landlord
entities.description: A letter listing all problems and history of previous attempts to get them solved, sent by certified mail, return receipt requested.
entities.source_quote: You must send the letter by certified mail, return receipt requested, and keep a copy of the letter, along with proof of mailing and the receipt, when it arrives, with your copy of the letter (along with the Tenants’ Inspection Checklist, if applicable) to show the HCR or a Housing Court judge, if you wind up going to court.

entities.name: Tenants’ Inspection Checklist
entities.description: A checklist to document all problems in the apartment and building to ensure nothing is left out of the initial written communication.
entities.source_quote: you may want to download and print our Tenants’ Inspection Checklist/Lista de verificación de la inspección de los inquilinos (公寓居住條件調查問卷) and use it as a guide so that you don’t leave anything out.

entities.name: Photos of Violations
entities.description: Pictures of any violations that can be seen, to be taken to court as evidence.
entities.source_quote: Take new pictures of any violations that can be seen.

entities.name: HPD Violation List
entities.description: Printout of the list of violations for the building and apartment from HPD's website.
entities.source_quote: Print out the list of violations for your building and apartment from HPD’s website: nyc.gov/hpd.

entities.name: Proof of Mailings
entities.description: Proof of mailing letters to the landlord, such as certified mail receipts.
entities.source_quote: Take to court all the evidence you have to prove your case: letters, proof of mailings, photos, heat logs, notices from your landlord, and witnesses, if necessary.

entities.name: City Agency Violation Report
entities.description: A copy of a violation report from a city agency (like HPD) for issues like no heat or hot water, required to file with HCR.
entities.source_quote: If the problem is no heat or hot water, you can file once you have a copy of a violation report from a city agency.

entities.name: Filing an HP Action in Housing Court
entities.description: Step-by-step process for tenants to sue their landlord in Housing Court for failure to make repairs.

steps: Go to the Housing Court in your borough and ask for paperwork to start a Housing Part (HP) Action.
Get help filling out forms at clerks’ windows, Court Help Center, or Housing Court Answers.
File the forms with the clerk (fee may be waived for low income).
You will be given a court date and an inspection date.
Serve your landlord and the city with a copy of the papers as instructed by the clerk.
Be at home on the inspection date and return to court on the specified date.

entities.name: Filing a Complaint with City Agencies via 311
entities.description: Process for filing a complaint with city housing, building, and health code enforcement agencies.

steps: Request an inspection from the relevant department (HPD, DOB, DOH, DEP).
Always ask for and get the complaint number.
Call a local elected official to help with follow-up if needed.
For building-wide conditions, encourage all tenants to call every day until fixed.

entities.name: Applying for Rent Reduction with HCR
entities.description: Process for rent-stabilized/controlled tenants to file for a rent reduction due to decreased services.

steps: Write a letter to the landlord complaining about the problem and send by certified mail, return receipt requested.
Wait 10 days (but no more than 60 days) after sending the letter.
File complaint with HCR using form RA-81 (individual) or RA-84 (tenant group), available online or by calling 833-499-0343.
For no heat/hot water, file once you have a city agency violation report.

entities.name: Court Order to Correct Violations
entities.description: Most HP Actions end with the landlord signing an order to correct violations listed on the inspection report, with arrangements for access dates.

entities.name: HPD Ordered Repairs
entities.description: In extreme cases where a landlord repeatedly ignores court orders, a judge can order HPD to do the repairs and bill the landlord.

entities.name: HCR Rent Reduction and Freeze
entities.description: HCR has the power to order a rent reduction and rent freeze for rent-regulated apartments until the landlord fixes the problem.

entities.name: Rent Abatement
entities.description: Tenants may be entitled to a reduction in the amount of rent owed (rent abatement) if they can document not getting repairs or essential services.

entities.name: New York Housing Maintenance Laws
entities.description: Laws and regulations of New York require that landlords maintain essential services and make prompt repairs.

section_b.chunk_text_preview: Getting Repairs - Met Council on Housing Skip to content The Basics of Getting RepairsThe laws and regulations of New York require that your landlord maintain essential services in your building and apartment and that they make prompt repairs.
